➜ proactive-context git:(master) ✗ pc archeologist archeologist: finishing current session (up to ~5m), then exiting… archeologist: complete — 0 captured / 1 seen (0 triage-skip, 1 too-short), 0 guides, 0 statements, 0 revisions, 0 removals, 0 links, 2m17s in all those 2m I didn't see a single log update, I had no idea what the thing was doing -- I want it to tell me what its going on more proactively

## What I found

Your run wasn't actually idle — it was doing a lot, just silently. The recording (`~/.proactive-context/logs/archeologist.stderr` + `events.jsonl`) shows the real per-session pipeline is:

`capture.start` → `llm.request` → **[one slow EXTRACT call, ~2 min, dead silent]** → `capture.extract` → `capture.authority_tagging` → `capture.route` → per-guide reconcile (`wiki.*`) → `capture.agent_done` → `capture.done`

The TUI tails `events.jsonl` but its feed only rendered `wiki.*` mutations, and the "current" box said a generic `(mining the past)`. So during the dominant cost — a single multi-minute LLM call — nothing on screen moved. The pasted `0 captured / 1 too-short` was a second lie: you quit mid-EXTRACT, so the session reached `capture.start` but the loop stopped before folding `capture.done`, and `seen − captured − triage_skip` mislabeled an interrupted capture as "too-short."

## What I changed (all in `src/archeologist.rs`, TUI path only)

1. **Live phase in the headline** — the "current" box now narrates the actual stage (`starting` → `extracting claims` → `tagging authority` → `routing to guides` → `reconciling guides` → `writing wiki` → `rebuilding index`), driven by the session's own events, with a `· waiting on model` marker between an `llm.request` and its response. This is the piece that stays informative *through* a silent LLM call.
2. **Feed narrates the lifecycle** — added `capture.start / extract / authority_tagging / route / agent_done / done` feed lines, so each phase prints a line (e.g. `⛏ extract 12 raw claim(s)`, `🧭 route → 3 guide(s)`) instead of a blank feed until the first wiki write.
3. **Truthful summary** — added a `started` counter (`capture.start`), redefined `too_short = seen − started − triage_skip`, and added an `interrupted` bucket for sessions stopped mid-capture. The quit path now drains the trailing events after the worker finishes, so a session you `q`-interrupt (which still runs to completion and writes its wiki) reports as **captured**, not interrupted/too-short.

No `capture.rs` change was needed — all the events already exist in `events.jsonl`.

## Verification

Unit tests + clean build + an event-trace walkthrough (I confirmed `init_context(&project, &input.session_id)` at `capture.rs:2145` makes the replay's events carry the historical session id, so the headline gate matches). 80/80 tests pass. I did **not** do a live TUI run (interactive + real LLM spend) — worth one real `pc archeologist` run to confirm the feel.

Two things I deliberately left alone: the line-log path (`--yes`/non-TTY — you hit the TTY path), and persisting the feed to disk on exit (the `archeologist.stderr` trail already captures it). Say the word if you want either.

Done. All three complaints fixed, build clean, 86/86 tests pass.

## What I changed

**1. "Shows New guide a bunch of times, but no claims"** — `src/capture.rs`
The reconcile emitted `wiki.create` based on the op *label* (`op.op == "create"`), not on whether a guide was actually created. When the LLM labels every statement of one brand-new guide as "create" (your "Relay-Enforced Event Chains" ×10), the first genuinely creates it and the rest fall through to the add-statement path — but all still fired `wiki.create`. Now I detect *genuine* creation with a flag set inside the locked closure (`existing.is_none()`), and skip the event entirely on error. The 10 dup "New guide" lines collapse to **1 New guide + 9 claim lines** — fixing both halves at once (the math checks out: 1 create + 9 adds + 1 REPL op = the "11 op(s)" you saw).

**2. Select "Reading conversation" → see the transcript sent to the LLM** — `src/archeologist.rs`
Enter on a "Reading conversation" line now shows the full EXTRACT transcript (the line-numbered conversation we sent the model), rendered role-by-role, in a **scrollable** overlay (↑/↓; the old overlay silently clipped long content).

A subtlety I had to dig out from the recording: every LLM call in a session **reuses one sidecar filename** (`req_id` is fixed per session, `turn` stays 1), so a later reconcile call overwrites EXTRACT's file ~20s after it's written. Storing the path and reading it later would show the *wrong* prompt. So I capture the transcript **eagerly** the moment the EXTRACT `llm.response` event arrives — the ~20s overwrite gap vs the TUI's 100ms drain makes that safe. Verified the JSON shape (`request.messages`) against a real sidecar on disk.

**3. Selection "stops rendering rows under the current row"** — `src/archeologist.rs`
The window's bottom edge was tied to the scroll offset (`end = total - feed_scroll`), so every Up peeled a row off the bottom and the cursor was actually *outside* the rendered window. Rewrote it as a bottom-anchored window where the cursor moves *within* the window and only scrolls it once the cursor climbs above the top. Unified the cursor-index math into one `feed_cursor_idx` helper used by render, Enter, and scroll so they can't drift.

## Not done (deliberately)
- **No commit** — you haven't asked, and we're on `master`.
- **No live TUI run** — that's interactive and spends real LLM budget. The logic, the transcript parser (against a real file), and the overwrite timing are all verified, but the actual *feel* of selection/scroll/transcript is worth a real `pc archeologist` run to confirm.
- The **live `tui.rs` detail modal** likely has the same sidecar-overwrite latent bug (reads the path lazily → shows overwritten content for completed turns). Out of scope for these three complaints; flagged in memory. The root fix would be unique sidecar filenames, but that risks unbounded `llm_turns/` growth product-wide, so I left it.

Done. But **first — rebuild and re-run before judging it**, because there's a cheap explanation that would make all of this moot:

```
cargo build --bin pc && pc archeologist
```

I never ran the TUI live, so if your last run used a stale `pc` binary you'd have been seeing the *original* pre-fix bug regardless of my code. Rule that out first.

## What I found and changed

Tracing the windowing math, the static behavior is actually correct — the cursor stays inside the rendered window and the container scrolls once the cursor reaches the top edge. I **proved** that with a new invariant test (`feed_window_always_keeps_the_cursor_visible`) so it stops being the suspect. The real culprit was the **live-update path**:

**1. Position drift while scrolled up** (`src/archeologist.rs:1457`) — the old code only held your scroll position when *paused* (`if view.feed_paused && !was_at_bottom`). So if you scrolled up during a live run without pausing, every incoming event grew the feed but left `feed_scroll` unchanged — the view slid out from under you and the selection drifted to a different line. Now: `if view.feed_paused || !was_at_bottom`, bumping `feed_scroll` in lock-step with the growing feed so the cursor stays on the same logical line. Live-follow resumes when you scroll back to the bottom. (Verified the ring-cap case too: when the buffer drops an old row, the `+1` still keeps the cursor on the right item.)

**2. No position readout** ("I don't see the position") — the feed title now shows `feed · line 47/210` whenever you're scrolled up, so you can see exactly where the cursor is. This helps **whether or not** the run is live.

**3. Refactor** — extracted the window math into a testable `feed_window()` helper used by render, locked by the invariant test above.

One honest caveat: fix #1 only bites *during* a live run. If you hit this *after* the run finished, the drift fix changes nothing — but fix #2 (the indicator) covers that case. If after rebuilding you still can't track the cursor, tell me whether it's during or after the run and I'll narrow further.
